payload.abstract_signature: Large language models frequently fail to integrate necessary contextual information when applying logical rules, leading to incorrect conclusions even when the underlying inference rule is known. Accurate logical reasoning requires the simultaneous processing of explicit premises and implicit contextual constraints without overlooking negations or scope limitations.
payload.argument: Large language models frequently fail to integrate necessary contextual information when applying logical rules, leading to incorrect conclusions even when the underlying inference rule is known. Accurate logical reasoning requires the simultaneous processing of explicit premises and implicit contextual constraints without overlooking negations or scope limitations.
payload.canonical_key: large language models frequently fail to integrate necessary contextual information when applying logical rules leading to incorrect conclusions even when underlying inference rule
payload.discussion: This finding highlights a fundamental limitation in current transformer-based architectures where attention mechanisms may prioritize salient keywords over logical operators like negation. For frameworks aiming to extract logical patterns during inference, this suggests that mere exposure to exemplars is insufficient; the extraction mechanism must explicitly enforce attention to logical connectives and contextual boundaries. Ignoring this sensitivity leads to brittle systems that fail under slight perturbations in premise formulation.
payload.evidence: Experimental analysis across multiple models (GPT-4, ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-2, Mistral) using the LogicBench dataset reveals that performance drops significantly in instances involving negations and complex reasoning structures. The models often overlook specific contextual details required to validate the applicability of an inference rule, resulting in hallucinated or logically invalid conclusions despite correct chain-of-thought prompting.
payload.name: Contextual Information Sensitivity in Logical Deduction
payload.problem_pressure: I would like to design a framework that lets LLMs extract logical patterns from other exemplars during inference and use the logical patterns to improve the reasoning accuracy-token frontier.
payload.source_paper_title: LogicBench: Towards Systematic Evaluation of Logical Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models

payload.canonical_key: current large language models exhibit substantial performance deficits on non monotonic reasoning tasks and instances involving negation compared to standard propositional logic ta
payload.condition: The result holds when evaluating models on natural language datasets that strictly isolate single inference rules, specifically those involving default reasoning with irrelevant information, reasoning with unknown expectations, and propositional rules containing negative literals.
payload.discussion: The empirical lesson is that robust logical reasoning frameworks cannot rely solely on emergent abilities from pre-training. Instead, they require targeted fine-tuning or inference-time constraints that explicitly model default logic semantics and enforce rigorous negation handling to prevent hallucinated or logically invalid conclusions.
payload.evidence: Experimental results show that existing LLMs do not fare well on LogicBench; especially, they struggle with instances involving complex reasoning and negations, and sometimes overlook contextual information necessary for reasoning to arrive at the correct conclusion.
payload.finding: Current large language models exhibit substantial performance deficits on non-monotonic reasoning tasks and instances involving negation compared to standard propositional logic tasks. Even advanced models like GPT-4 struggle to maintain accuracy when the reasoning path requires handling default assumptions or contradictory information.
payload.main_results: Current large language models exhibit substantial performance deficits on non-monotonic reasoning tasks and instances involving negation compared to standard propositional logic tasks. Even advanced models like GPT-4 struggle to maintain accuracy when the reasoning path requires handling default assumptions or contradictory information.

work_version_records.abstract: Recently developed large language models (LLMs) have been shown to perform remarkably well on a wide range of language understanding tasks. But, can they really "reason" over the natural language? This question has been receiving significant research attention and many reasoning skills such as commonsense, numerical, and qualitative have been studied. However, the crucial skill pertaining to 'logical reasoning' has remained underexplored. Existing work investigating this reasoning ability of LLMs has focused only on a couple of inference rules (such as modus ponens and modus tollens) of propositional and first-order logic. Addressing the above limitation, we comprehensively evaluate the logical reasoning ability of LLMs on 25 different reasoning patterns spanning over propositional, first-order, and non-monotonic logics. To enable systematic evaluation, we introduce LogicBench, a natural language question-answering dataset focusing on the use of a single inference rule. We conduct detailed analysis with a range of LLMs such as GPT-4, ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-2, and Mistral using chain-of-thought prompting. Experimental results show that existing LLMs do not fare well on LogicBench; especially, they struggle with instances involving complex reasoning and negations. Furthermore, they sometimes overlook contextual information necessary for reasoning to arrive at the correct conclusion. We believe that our work and findings facilitate future research for evaluating and enhancing the logical reasoning ability of LLMs. Data and code are available at https://github.com/Mihir3009/LogicBench.

metadata.title: LogicBench: Towards Systematic Evaluation of Logical Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models

payload.abstract: Recently developed large language models (LLMs) have been shown to perform remarkably well on a wide range of language understanding tasks. But, can they really "reason" over the natural language? This question has been receiving significant research attention and many reasoning skills such as commonsense, numerical, and qualitative have been studied. However, the crucial skill pertaining to 'logical reasoning' has remained underexplored. Existing work investigating this reasoning ability of LLMs has focused only on a couple of inference rules (such as modus ponens and modus tollens) of propositional and first-order logic. Addressing the above limitation, we comprehensively evaluate the logical reasoning ability of LLMs on 25 different reasoning patterns spanning over propositional, first-order, and non-monotonic logics. To enable systematic evaluation, we introduce LogicBench, a natural language question-answering dataset focusing on the use of a single inference rule. We conduct detailed analysis with a range of LLMs such as GPT-4, ChatGPT, Gemini, Llama-2, and Mistral using chain-of-thought prompting. Experimental results show that existing LLMs do not fare well on LogicBench; especially, they struggle with instances involving complex reasoning and negations. Furthermore, they sometimes overlook contextual information necessary for reasoning to arrive at the correct conclusion. We believe that our work and findings facilitate future research for evaluating and enhancing the logical reasoning ability of LLMs. Data and code are available at https://github.com/Mihir3009/LogicBench.
